Facts.text: Duke of Austria Albert V orders the Vienna Gesera, imprisoning and expelling the Jews of Vienna
Pope Paul III installs Portuguese inquisition
Battle of Heiligerlee, Groningen: Dutch rebels beat Spanish, thousands killed
The Netherlands declares independence from Spain
Official ratification of the Second Charter of Virginia takes place
Matthias von Habsburg chosen as King of Bohemia

Second Defenestration of Prague: Two Catholic Lords Regent and their secretary are thrown out of a window and amazingly are not seriously injured by the 70 foot (21m) fall. Triggers the Thirty Years' War.
Johan Mauritius van Nassau resigns as head of Civil rights activists
William II Prince of Orange sworn in as Stadtholder of Holland
King Afonso VI of Portugal flees
Chestertown tea party occurs (tea dumped into Chester River)
Declaration of Bab (Baha'i festival) ('Azamat 7, 1)
New York City Police Department (NYPD) is formed, replacing an old night watch system
Buenos Aires gains independence from Argentina (reunited 1859)
Three Virginia slaves Frank Baker, Shepard Mallory and James Townsend escape, rowing across Hampton Roads to Fort Monroe, Virginia - declared 1st contraband of war the next day
Virginia citizens vote 3 to 1 in favor of secession from the Union
Organization of the Seventh-day Adventist Church in Battle Creek, Michigan
Battle of North Anna, Virginia, 1st of 3 days of fighting
-24] Victory parade in Washington, D.C. (Grand Review)
Flag flown at full mast over White House for the first time since Lincoln was shot
1st Preakness Stakes: G. Barbee aboard Survivor wins in 2:43 at Pimlico Race Course, Baltimore, Maryland
Canada's North West Mounted Police Force forms (receives the "Royal" designation in 1904)
Postal cards sold in San Francisco for 1st time
Attorney John Henry Smyth named US minister to Liberia
6" of snow falls in eastern Iowa
1st transcontinental train arrives in Vancouver, British Columbia
William Love hosts groundbreaking ceremonies for Love Canal, a planned community on shores of Lake Ontario
1st Philippine Expeditionary Troops sail from San Francisco
William Harvey Carney becomes the first African-American soldier awarded the Medal of Honor for his actions in the Civil War at the Battle of Fort Wagner
Ottawa Mint Act receives Royal Assent
1st automobile trip across US leaves San Francisco for New York, (arrives July 26)
1st direct primary election law in US adopted, by Wisconsin
The single chamber Parliament of Finland gathers for its first plenary session.
Dirigible explodes over San Francisco Bay, 16 passengers fall, none killed
Dutch socialist Henk Sneevliet co-founds The Indies Social Democratic Association (Communist Party) in Dutch Indonesia
Italy declares war on Austria-Hungary during WW I
Dutch 2nd Chamber approves 1908 conscription draft
King Oil/Shell refinery on Curacao officially opens
Pope Benedictus XV publishes encyclical Pacem Dei
Comedic play "Abie's Irish Rose" premieres at Fulton Theatre, NYC; runs for 2,327 performances (longest run at the time)
Future World Heavyweight boxing champion Gene Tunney suffers his only professional defeat in 15-round unanimous points decision against Harry Greb at Madison Square Garden, NY
Launch of Belgium's SABENA airline: first flight from Brussels to Lympne, England
Lebanese constitution forms under French mandate
Anarchist bomb attack on Italian Consulate in Buenos Aires, Argentina kills 7 and injures dozens
Whipsnade Zoo opens in Bedfordshire, England
Australian cyclist Hubert Opperman sets paced world records 1,384km in 24 hours, and 1,609km (1,000 miles) in 28 hours, 55 minutes, 39 seconds at Melbourne Motordrome
The Auto-Lite Strike culminates in the "Battle of Toledo", a five-day melée between 1,300 troops of the Ohio National Guard and 6,000 picketers
1st scheduled MLB baseball night game postponed due to rain  in Cincinnati, Ohio
British parliament plans to make Palestine independent by 1949

Submarine USS Squalus sinks in the Gulf of Maine, drowning 26, 33 remaining crew rescued from a depth of 243 ft (74 m) by divers using newly developed heliox air systems (divers later awarded the Medal of Honor)
1st great dogfight between Spitfires and Luftwaffe
-24] 826 Allied bombers attack Dortmund
British and Canadian troops occupy Pontecorvo, Italy
Chinese counter offensive at Hunan front
Operation Buffalo: Allied break out from Anzio bridgehead
German island of Helgoland in the North Sea surrenders to British
PC Hooft prize forms for literature
Ramat Rahel gateway to Jerusalem is repossessed by Israel
Federal Republic of (West) Germany is created from the American, British, and French occupation zones
Seventeen Point Agreement for the Peaceful Liberation of Tibet signed in Beijing, formally establishes Chinese sovereignty over Tibet
WHIZ TV channel 18 in Zanesville, OH (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
World Trade Center dedicated in Ferry Building, San Francisco
Explorer 1 ceases transmission - 1st US satellite launched into space January 1958
US schools 1st use Cliff's Notes
"Got A Girl" by The Four Preps hits #24
WGTV TV channel 8 in Athens-Atlanta, GA (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKBM TV (now WLII) channel 11 in Caguas/San Juan, PR 1st broadcast
WRCA radio changes call letters back to WNBC (NYC)
15th Cannes Film Festival: "Keeper of Promises" directed by Anselmo Duarte wins the Palme d'Or
French OAS leader general Raoul Salan sentenced to life in prison (pardoned 1968)
Joe Pepitone2nd NY Yankee to hit 2 HRs in 1 inning (Joe DiMaggio)
NBC purchases 1963 AFL championship game TV rights for $926,000
Dale Greig runs female marathon world record (3:27:45)
Franz Jonas elected President of Austria
Pontoon ferry overturned on Shire River, Malawi, kills 150
Te Arikinui, Dame Te Atairangikaahu is chosen as the first Maori queen, succeeding her father King Koroki
Government bans submarines near South Africa
Beatles open 2nd Apple Boutique at 161 New Kings Road, London
22nd Cannes Film Festival: "If..." directed by Lindsay Anderson wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
Lauwerszee Dike in Holland closes
Track Records releases The Who's double album rock opera "Tommy" in the UK
A fire breaks out in the Britannia Bridge over the Menai Straits in north Wales contributing to its partial destruction and causing approximately £1,000,000 worth of fire damage
Grateful Dead's 1st performance outside US, as part of 'The Hollywood Rock Music Festival', in Newcastle under Lyme, England
San Diego Padres beat SF Giants 17-16 in 15 innings
USSR performs nuclear test (underground)
American rock group Iron Butterfly ("In-A-Gadda-Da-Vida") disbands
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Italian Red Brigade officer Mario Sossi freed
28th Cannes Film Festival: "Chronicle of the Years of Fire" directed by Mohammed Lakhdar-Hamina wins the Palme d'Or
Benin adopts its constitution
Moluccan extremists hold 105 schoolchildren and 50 others hostage on a hijacked train in the Netherlands, children released May 27, siege ends June 11
AL approves transfer of Red Sox to Jean Yawkey for $15M
General strike in Peru
1st edition of "Wisden Cricket Monthly"
West-Germany elects Karl Carstens president
ABC Masters Bowling Tournament won by Neil Burton
Barcelona fascists take 200 people hostage
NASA launches Intelsat V satellite, no. 501
BBC warns Britain will bomb Argentina
Colin Wilson rides a surfboard 294 miles
Radio Moscow announcer Vladimir Danchev praises Afghanistan Muslims standing up to Russia; he is removed from the air
Detroit Tigers win AL record tying 16th straight road game
US & Western Europe veto heavier sanctions against South Africa
41st Cannes Film Festival: "Pelle erobreren" directed by Bille August wins the Palme d'Or
Maryland stops sale of cheap pistols on Jan 1, 1990
Cleveland loses and drop to 21-22, this is the latest a sub .500 team is in 1st place (AL East)
Lincoln Square in Bronx named
Cost of rescuing savings & loan failures is put at up to $130 billion
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits a record 2,856.26
NY Yankees hit 6 home runs to beat Minnesota Twins 12-0
Last Cubans troops leave Angola
Philadelphia Phillies pitcher Tommy Greene no-hits Montreal Expos, 2-0
San Diego Sockers win 4th consecutive Major Soccer League championship
US Supreme Court bars subsidized clinics from discussing abortion
NY Yankees play in their 4th straight extra inning game
Sicilian Mafia bombs motorcade killing anti-mob prosecutor Giovanni Falcone, his wife, and three police escorts on Highway A29, near Capaci, Sicily
"Star Trek: The Next Generation" finale airs this week in syndication
270 pilgrims die in the bustle round Mina, Saudi-Arabia
Roman Herzog elected President of Germany
47th time opposing pitchers hit HRs, K Foster (Cubs)/M Freeman (Rocks)
Fred Norris of the Howard Stern show changes his first name legally to Eric
"King David" closes at New Amsterdam Theater NYC
Mel Karmazin replaces Peter Lund as CEO of CBS TV
The Good Friday Agreement is accepted in a referendum in Northern Ireland with 75% voting yes
52nd Cannes Film Festival: "Rosetta" directed by Jean-Pierre and Luc Dardenne wins the Palme d'Or
Marco Siffredi becomes the first person to snowboard down Mount Everest via the Norton Couloir (some share record accreditation with Stefan Gatt)
The "55 parties" clause of the Kyoto protocol is reached after its ratification by Iceland
The Euro exceeds its initial trading value as it hits $1.18 for the first time since its introduction in 1999
Part of Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport's Terminal 2E collapses, killing four people and injuring three others.
International Court of Justice awards Middle Rocks to Malaysia and Pedra Branca (Pulau Batu Puteh) to Singapore, ending a 29-year territorial dispute
63rd Cannes Film Festival: "Uncle Boonmee Who Can Recall His Past Lives" directed by Apichatpong Weerasethakul wins the Palme d'Or
Jason Koumassigns a one-year extension with Tranmere Rovers
Russia and China veto the U.N. Security Council resolution to establish an International Criminal Court for war crimes in Syria
Chinese archaeologists announce findings of the earliest use of barley in China to make beer, in Shaanxi Province between 3400 and 2900 BC
Library of Al-Qarawiyyin, the world's oldest continually operating library, reopens after a major restoration by King Mohammed VI in Fez, Morocco
Michael Bayis honored with a hand-and-footprint ceremony at The TCL Chinese Theatre
UK raises terror threat level to critical following Manchester bombing
Hamburg, Germany, becomes the first city to ban diesel cars on some roads
NFL owners approve new NFL national anthem policy whereby players required to stand if they choose to be on the field for pre-game presentations
Brazilian cosmetics group Natura buys UK's Avon for $2 billion, creating the world's fourth-largest cosmetics company
Fifty children rescued from an international paedophile ring on the dark web in Thailand, Australia and the US by Interpol under Operation Blackwrist, main organizer sentenced to 146 years
More than 170 tornadoes reported in a week in US states of Missouri, Oklahoma and Iowa, killing seven and causing widespread damage
Prototype of new high-speed train that will float above the track, capable of travelling 600km an hour (370 mph), unveiled by Chinese Railway Rolling Stock Corporation in Qingdao
Six migrant children have now died in US custody in eight months prompting calls for an investigation
The last slave ship to smuggle slaves to America from Africa, the Clotilda (sunk 1860), is found in Mobile river, Alabama
21 runners die in freezing conditions during an ultramarathon in Yellow River Stone Forest Park, Gansu Province, China
Belarus accused of “state-sponsored hijacking" after diverting commercial Ryanair flight to Minsk to arrest dissident journalist Roman Protasevich
New York City removes its last public payphones in Midtown Manhattan, they will become part of a museum display
US PresidentJoe Bidensays for the first time he would be willing to use force to defend Taiwan against a Chinese invasion
451 Illinois Catholic clergy sexually abused nearly 2,000 children over 70 years, according to state's attorney general’s office
International Booker Prize awarded to "Time Shelter" by Bulgarian writer Georgi Gospodinov, translated by Angela Rodel is announced in London
PresidentJoe Bidensays he will work to make Kenya the US's first key non-NATO ally during a state visit by Kenyan President William Ruto to the White House
Battle of Dandanaqan:Tughril's Seljuk army defeats Ghaznavid force, near Merv (present-day Turkmenistan), brings about the fall of the Ghaznavid Empire
Henri I crowns his son KingPhilip I(called the Amorous) of France
KingEdward Iof England orders cessation of persecution of French Jews
Joan of Arcis captured at Compiegne and sold to the English
King of FranceCharles VIII, King of the RomansMaximilian Iand Archduke of Austria Philip I sign the Treaty of Senlis, ending hostilities between France and the Seventeen Provinces
PopeJulius IIand French troops capture Bologna after an anti-Papal revolt
News reaches Cairo and the Mamluk Empire, causing panic, of the first Portuguese fleet to travel up the Red Sea, led byAfonso de Albuquerque
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vrecognizes KingChristian IIIof Denmark
Giampietro Caraffa elected PopePaul IV
King Frederick of Denmark offers island of Hveen to astronomer and noblemanTycho Braheto build an observatory
KingCharles IIreturns from exile sails from Scheveningen to England
CaptainWilliam Kiddis hanged in London after being convicted of piracy and the murder of William Moore
Battle of Ramillies during War of the Spanish Succession.:John Churchill(1st Duke of Marlborough) defeats French; 17,000 killed
Carlo Goldoni's play "Il Bugiardo" (The Liar) premieres in Mantua
Benjamin Franklinannounces his invention of bifocals
South American independence leaderSimón Bolívarenters Mérida, leading the invasion of Venezuela, and is proclaimed El Libertador (The Liberator)
Valley Campaign:Stonewall Jacksontakes Fort Royal, Virginia
Jesse James' gang robs a bank in Richmond, Missouri, killing two and taking $4,000
US captures leader of Filipino rebels,Emilio Aguinaldo
NY Public Library building at 5th Avenue dedicated by PresidentWilliam Howard Taft
Walt Disneyincorporates his 1st film company Laugh-O-Gram Films
Chicago Cub'sHack Wilsonis 1st to hit a home run off Wrigley Field scoreboard
Adolf Hitlerproclaims he wants to move into Poland
ComposerDmitri Shostakovichappointed professor at conservatory of Leningrad
In his 20th World Heavyweight Boxing title defenseJoe Louisknocks out Buddy Baer in round 1 at New York's Madison Square Garden
Thomas Mannbegins writing his novel Dr Faustus
Lord Haw-Haw (William Joyce) arrested at Danish boundary
The Allies arrest the members of the Nazi Flensburg government, including AdmiralKarl Dönitz, formally dissolving Nazi Germany
Future Baseball Hall of Fame center fielderJoe DiMaggiohits 3 consecutive HRs as the New York Yankees edge the Indians, 6-5 at Cleveland Stadium
Peter Ustinov's play "Love of Four Colonels" premieres in London
Mao Zedongstarts the "Great Leap Forward" movement in China, kills between 23 and 55 million Chinese citizens due to famine and forced labor
16th Cannes Film Festival: "The Leopard" directed by Luchino Visconti wins the Palme d'Or
BBC orders 13 episodes ofMonty Python's Flying Circus
US Supreme Court refuses to hear appeals of Watergate wrongdoersH. R. Haldeman, John Ehrlichman & John Mitchell
American rock singer-songwriterTom Pettyfiles for chapter 11 bankruptcy
33rd Cannes Film Festival: "All That Jazz" directed byBob Fosseand "Kagemusha" directed byAkira Kurosawajointly awarded the Palme d'Or
Horror film "The Shining" is released, directed byStanley Kubrickand starringJack NicholsonandShelley Duvall, based on the book byStephen King
Peter Sutcliffeis convicted for the "Yorkshsire Ripper" murders of 13 women at the Old Bailey in London and sentenced to life sentences for each
Puerto Rican boxerWilfred Benítez(22) becomes the youngest 3-division world champion in history by knocking out WBC World Super Welterweight champion Maurice Hope in 12 rounds in Las Vegas
PopeJohn Paul IIbeatifies Dutch priest and missionary Peter Donders
"Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom", 2nd in the film series, directed bySteven Spielberg, starringHarrison Ford, and produced byGeorge Lucas' Lucosfilms premieres
37th Cannes Film Festival: "Paris, Texas" directed by Wim Wenders wins the Palme d'Or, starringHarry Dean Stantonand Nastassja Kinski
Epic crime film "Once Upon a Time in America" directed bySergio LeonestarringRobert De NiroandJames Woodspremieres at Cannes Film Festival
PresidentRonald ReaganawardsJimmy Stewartthe Presidential Medal of Freedom and promotes him to Major General on the Retired List
42nd Cannes Film Festival: "Sex, Lies, and Videotape" directed bySteven Soderberghwins the Palme d'Or
US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushorders Coast Guard to intercept boats with Haitian refugees
47th Cannes Film Festival: "Pulp Fiction" directed byQuentin Tarantinowins the Palme d'Or
Eminemreleases his third studio album "The Marshall Mathers LP," the fastest-selling studio album ever (Grammy - Best Rap Album, 2001)
American singer-songwriterPaul Simonreceives Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the Warner Theatre in Washington, D.C.
Patrick Royis named head coach of the Colorado Avalanche, his former team where he won two Stanley Cups
"Pauline" a chamber opera byMargaret Atwoodand Tobin Stokes premieres at the York Theatre in Vancouver
US PresidentDonald TrumpmeetsPope Francisat the Vatican

Indian Prime MinisterNarendra Modi's Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) wins re-election in a landslide over the opposition Congress Party, world's largest-ever democratic election with over 600 million voters
US PresidentJoe Bidenlaunches the Indo-Pacific Economic Framework in Tokyo, an economic alliance of Asia-Pacific nations to counter Chinese influence, including India, South Korea, Japan, and others
Apple Music namesLauryn Hill's "The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill" the best album ever made
UK Prime MinisterRishi Sunakcalls an early general election for the 4th of July
South Carolina becomes 8th state to ratify US constitution
Part of Great White Fleet arrives in Puget Sound, Washington
American outlaws Bonnie Parker andClyde Barroware killed by police in an ambush near Sailes, Louisiana
Israel announces capture of NaziAdolf Eichmannin Argentina
Qinzong, last Emperor of Song Dynasty in China, born in China (d. 1161)
Claude Mellan, French engraver, cartoonist and painter, born in Abbeville, France (d. 1688)
Juan Caramuel y Lobkowitz, Spanish writer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1682)
Elias Ashmole, English antiquarian and collector (Ashmolean Museum), born in Litchfield (d. 1692)
Pieter Neefs, the Younger, Flemish painter (church interiors), baptized in Antwerp (d. 1675 or after)
Thomas Eisenhut, German Kapellmeister, organist, musicologist and priest, born in Ausburg, Ele ctorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1702)
Johann Caspar Vogler, German composer and organist, born in Hausen, Schwarzburg-Arnstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1763)
François Gaspard Adam, French sculptor (garden sculptures), born in Nancy, France (d. 1761)
William Hunter, Scottish anatomist and physician, born in East Kilbride, South Lanarkshire, Scotland (d. 1783)
Giuseppe Parini, Italian prose writer and poet (Il Giorno), born in Bosisio, Lombardy, Italy (d. 1799)
Franz Anton Mesmer, Austrian physician and hypnotist (Mesmerism), born in Iznang, Germany (d. 1815)
Charles Joseph, 7th Prince the Ligne, Belgian fieldmarshal and author, born in Brussels (d. 1814)
Louis François Chambray, French composer, born in Château de Chambray, Evreux, France (d. 1807)
Andrea Lucchesi, Italian organist, composer, and Kapellmeister for the Court of Bonn (1774-94), born in Motta di Livenza,Rebuplic of Venice (d. 1801)
Andrea Appiani, Italian royal painter (Napoleon), born in Milan (d. 1817)
Nicolas-Joseph Hüllmandel, Alsatian composer, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1823)
António da Silva Leite, Portuguese composer, born in Porto, Portugal (d. 1833)
Jules Dumont d'Urville, French explorer and naval officer, born in Condé-sur-Noireau, France (d. 1842)
(Isaac) Ignaz Moscheles, Bohemian piano virtuoso, friend of Beethoven, teacher of Mendelssohn, conductor, and composer, born in Prague, Bohemia (now Czech Republic) (d. 1870)[1]
Charles Barry, British architect, born in London (d. 1860)
Thomas Hood, English poet and composer (The Song of the Shirt), born in London (d. 1845)
Henri-François-Alphonse Esquiros, French poet and writer (Les Vierges Folles), born in Paris (d. 1876)
Mason Brayman, American attorney, newspaperman and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1895)
James Buchanan Eads, American engineer and inventor (Eads Bridge-St Louis), born in Lawrenceburg, Indiana (d. 1887)
Lorenzo Sawyer, 9th Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of California, born in Le Roy, New York (d. 1891)
Edward Hitchcock Jr., 1st American physical educator (Amherst College), born in Amherst, Massachusetts (d. 1911)
Pieter Jan Baptist Carel Robidé van der Aa, Dutch Indologist and geographer, born in Oosterbeek, Netherlands (d. 1887)
Carl Heinrich Bloch, Danish painter (art for King's Chapel at Frederiksborg Palace), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1890)
James Sanks Brisbin, American educator, lawyer, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Boalsburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1892)
Józef Wieniawski, Polish pianist, composer, conductor and teacher, born in Lublin, Poland (d. 1912)
ʻAbdu'l-Bahá, Persian successor and head of the Bahá'í Faith (1892-1921) from his father Baháʼu'lláh, born in Tehran, Sublime State of Persia (d. 1921)
Helmuth von Moltke, German army general (German Chief of Staff at outbreak of WWI), born in Gersdorff, Mecklenburg (d. 1916)
Maritcha Remond Lyons, American educator, civic leader, suffragist, and civil rights leader (co-founder of the Women's Loyal Union of New York and Brooklyn), born in New York City (d. 1929)
Otto Lilienthal, German pioneer aviator who explored winged flight through gliders, born in Anklam, Province of Pomerania, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1896)
Antoni Stolpe, Polish pianist and composer, born in Pulawy, Poland (d. 1872)
William "Dummy" Hoy, American baseball outfielder (most accomplished deaf player in MLB history; NL stolen base leader 1888; Washington Senators, Cincinnati Reds), born in Houcktown, Ohio (d. 1961)
Epitácio Pessoa, 11th President of Brazil (Minas Gerais Republican: 1919-22), born in Umbuzeiro, Paraíba, Empire of Brazil (d. 1942)
Gustav Aschaffenburg, German psychiatrist (pioneer in criminology and forensic pathology), born in Zweibrücken (d. 1944)
Sigurd Lie, Norwegian composer and conductor, born in Drammen (d. 1904)
Leo Baeck, German rabbi (President of World Union for Progressive Judaism), born in Lissa, German Empire (d. 1956)
Alfred P. Sloan, American long-time president, chairman and CEO of General Motors Corporation (1923-56), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1966)
Dezső Lauber, Hungarian competitive athlete (Ice skating, golf, tennis, cycling), and architect, born in Pecs, Hungary (d. 1966)
Artur de Sacadura Freire Cabral, Portuguese aviator (1st flight across the south Atlantic), born in Celorico da Beira, Portugal (d. 1924)
James Gleason, American writer and actor (Bishop's Wife, Flying Fool), born in New York City (d. 1959)
Corrado Gini, Italian statistician, sociologist, and developer of the Gini coefficient, born in Motta di Livenza, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1965)
Thoralf Skolem, Norwegian mathematician (mathematical logic and set theory), born in Sandsvær, Norway (d. 1963)
Adrian Roland Holst, Dutch poet (Raged & Tired), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Zack Wheat, American Baseball Hall of Fame outfielder (NL batting champion 1918; Brooklyn Superbas / Dodgers / Robins 1909–26), born in Hamilton, Missouri (d. 1972)
Ernst Niekisch, German writer and politician, born in Trebnitz, German Empire (d. 1967)
Virginia True Boardman, American actress of the silent era (A Blind Bargain; The Lady Lies), born in Fort Davis, Texas (d. 1971)
Herbert Marshall, English actor (Trouble in Paradise; The Little Foxes), born in London (d. 1996)
Pär Lagerkvist, Swedish poet, novelist, and playwright (Nobel Prize in Literature, 1951), born in Växjö, Sweden (d. 1974)
Rafael Moreno Aranzadi [Pichichi], Spanish soccer forward (5 caps; Athletic Bilbao), born in Bilbao, Spain (d. 1922)
Ulysses S. Grant IV, American geologist and paleontologist, born in Salem Center, New York (d. 1977)
Felix Steiner, German SS commander during the Nazi era, born in Stallupönen, German Empire (d. 1966)
Fred Guy, American jazz banjo player and guitarist (Joseph C. Smith's Orchestra; Duke Ellington's Washingtonians), born in Burkeville, Virginia (d. 1971)
Frank McHugh, American actor (Going My Way, Front Page, Gold Diggers 1935, Mighty Joe Young), born in Homestead, Pennsylvania (d. 1981)
Joseph H. Hazen, Hollywood lawyer (Warner Brothers), born in Kingston, New York (d. 1996)
Scott O'Dell, American author and recipient of the Hans Christian Anderson Award (1972), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1989)
Ralph Dunn, American actor (Mr Rudge-Norby), born in Titusville, Pennsylvania (d. 1968)
Edmund Rubbra, English composer (Morning Watch), born in Semilong, Northampton, England (d. 1986)
Mark Lothar [Lothar Hundertmark], German composer (The Story of the Lazy Bear), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1985)
Walter Reisch, Austrian-American director and screenwriter (Ninotchka, Gaslight, Titanic), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1983)
Hellmuth Christian Wolff, German composer, teacher, musicologist, and painter, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1988)
Kenneth Allen, English industrialist (WH Allen and Sons Ltd, helped form CBI and negotiate 40 hr week), born in Bedford, England (d. 2000)[1]
Max Abramovitz, American architect (Lincoln Center, UN Building), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2004)
(Benjamin) "Scatman" Crothers, American singer, musician ("I'd Rather Be a Hummingbird"), and actor (Chico and the Man - "Louie", The Shining), born in Terre Haute, Indiana (d. 1986)
Artie Shaw [Arthur Arshawsky], American gig 0bandleader, jazz clarinetist ("Begin the Beguine"), and actor, born in New York City (d. 2004)
Franz Kline, American abstract expressionist painter, born in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (d. 1962)
Hugh Casson, English architect (Festival of Britain director, President of the Royal Academy), born in London, England (d. 1999)
Margaret Wise Brown, American author of children's books (Goodnight Moon; The Runaway Bunny), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1952)[1]
Boris Kremenliev, Bulgarian-American composer (The Tell-Tale Heart), educator (UCLA, 1947-78), and ethnomusicologist (Bulgarian-Macedonian Folk Music), born in Razlog, Bulgaria (d. 1988)
Melvin M Payne, American scientific leader (National Geographic Society, 1932-88), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1990)
John Payne, American actor (The Restless Gun; Miracle on 34th Street), born in Roanoke, Virginia (d. 1989)
Marius Goring, British actor (Stairway to Heaven; The Red Shoes), born in Isle of Wight, England (d. 1998)
Samuel Churran, British physicist (invented scintillation counter, 1st Vice Chancellor Strathclyde University - UK's 1st technological university), born in Ballymena, Northern Ireland (d. 1998)
Alec Dickson, British founder of Voluntary Service Overseas (VSO), born in Ruislip, London (d. 1994)
Barbara Ward, English economist and writer (Only One Earth), born in Heworth, England (d. 1981)
Leo Lerman, American writer and editor (Condé Nast Publications), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Travis Kemp, British ballet dancer and teacher, born in Nottingham, England (d. 1995)
Clyde Wiegand, American physicist (co-discovered the antiproton), born in Long Beach, Washington (d. 1996)
Jean Françaix, French classical pianist, and composer, born in Le Mans, France (d. 1997)
Edward Norton Lorenz, American mathematician and meteorologist, born in West Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2008)
Dennis Compton, England cricket batsman and footballer (5,807 Test runs, Arsenal 1950 FA Cup), born in Hendon, England (d. 1997)
Robert "Bumps" Blackwell, American bandleader, songwriter, and record producer (Little Richard; Sam Cooke), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1985)
Betty Garrett, American stage and screen comedian, singer, dancer, and actress (All In The Family - "Irene"; Laverne & Shirley - "Edna"), born in St Joseph, Missouri (d. 2011)
Gayatri Devi, 3rd Maharani consort of Jaipur, India (1940-49), born in London, England (d. 2009)
Robert K.J.E. Antonissen, Belgian-born South African literary figure, born in Antwerp (d. 1972)
Ruth Fernández, Puerto Rican singer ("Cuando Vuelvas" - "When You Return"), and politician (Senate-at-large, 1973-81), born in Ponce, Puerto Rico (d. 2012)
William LeMassena, American stage and screen actor (All That Jazz; As The World Turns, 1985-92), born in Glen Ridge, New Jersey (d. 1993)
Helen O'Connell, American big-band singer (Jimmy Dorsey Orchestra - "Green Eyes"; "Anapola"), and TV host (Today Show, 1956-58), born in Lima, Ohio (d. 1993)[1]
Sid Melton [Sidney Meltzer], American actor (Green Acres; The Danny Thomas Show), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2011)

Humphrey Lyttelton, British jazz trumpeter and bandleader ("Bad Penny Blues"), and broadcaster (BBC2 - Best Of Jazz, 1967-2007; BBC4 - I'm Sorry I Haven't A Clue, 1972-2008), born in Eton, Buckinghamshire (d. 2008)
James Blish [pseudonym William Atheling, Jr], American-British, sci-fi author (Hugo, Star Trek Reader), born in East Orange, New Jersey (d. 1975)
Loren Tindall, American actor (Meet Me on Broadway, Girl of Limberlost), born in Hastings, Oklahoma (d. 1973)
Montague Modlyn, English radio and TV broadcaster, born in London, England (d. 1994)
Alicia de Larrocha, Spanish pianist (Orquesta Sinfonica), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 2009)
Clyde King, American baseball pitcher, coach, manager and executive (Brooklyn Dodgers; New York Yankees), born in Goldsboro, North Carolina (d. 2010)
